m01.title: What is AI-Driven Development?
m01.content: Code is no longer written line by line — it's described, iterated and validated. AI acts as the developer's technical co-author.

columns.heading: Copilots (Autocomplete)
items: Complete code in real time as you type
Learn from the context of the open file
E.g. GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, Codeium
Lower latency, lower autonomy

columns.heading: Autonomous Agents
items: Execute complete tasks without constant supervision
Can read, create and modify files
Chain tool calls (terminal, web)
E.g. Cascade in Windsurf, Devin, Aider

columns.heading: Chat-Based Coding
items: Conversation in natural language + code
Dev validates and pastes the result manually
Ideal for exploration and learning
E.g. Claude.ai, ChatGPT, Gemini

columns.heading: Current Limitations
items: Hallucinations: plausible but incorrect code
Context window: forgets the start of the repo
Security risks: credentials, injections
Over-reliance without critical review

m01.title: IDE, Agent and Model: three distinct layers
m01.content: The vibe coding stack has three clearly separated layers. Each can be swapped independently.

columns.heading: IDE
items: Development environment
Editor, terminal and UI where the dev interacts with the agent

columns.heading: Agent
items: Autonomous orchestrator
Decides actions, reads files and calls the model in a loop

columns.heading: Model
items: LLM Engine
Generates code and reasoning. Executes nothing on its own

m02.title: Windsurf: The IDE that reasons about your codebase
m02.content: Windsurf doesn't just complete code — it understands the whole project, runs commands and makes architecture decisions.

columns.heading: Multi-file Context
items: Reads the entire repository, not just the active file
Understands imports, dependencies and patterns

columns.heading: Terminal Actions
items: Runs npm install, git, curl… without leaving the IDE
The agent closes the feedback loop

columns.heading: Real-world use cases
items: Complete REST APIs, large-scale refactors
Test generation, PR reviews

m02.title: VS Code + GitHub Copilot: the open ecosystem
m02.content: VS Code with GitHub Copilot brings AI-assisted development to the world's most popular editor — with agent mode, multi-model support and deep extension integration.

columns.heading: Agent Mode
items: Copilot acts autonomously: reads files, runs commands, edits code
Iterative loop similar to Cascade but inside VS Code

columns.heading: Multi-model
items: Choose between GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet, Gemini and more
Switch models per task without changing IDE

columns.heading: Extension Ecosystem
items: Thousands of extensions: Docker, databases, linters, testing
MCP servers for custom tool integration

m03.title: From zero to a working project in one session
m03.content: Tip: always start with an architecture prompt before asking for code. It changes everything.

steps.label: Installation
steps.desc: Install VS Code and the GitHub Copilot extension. Sign in with your GitHub account and select a model (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet).

steps.label: Scaffolding
steps.desc: Open Copilot Chat in agent mode and ask: 'Create a Node/FastAPI project with Clean Architecture structure.'

steps.label: README
steps.desc: Auto-generate the README: description, setup instructions, environment variables.

steps.label: Base code
steps.desc: AI generates routes, models, services. You review the logic and the API contract.

m04.title: Version control when 80% of the code is written by AI
m04.content: AI generates code fast — without commit discipline, history becomes unmanageable. Git is still your safety net.

columns.heading: AI-oriented Git basics
items: git diff before every commit: review what the AI generated
Atomic commits: one concept per commit, not 'AI session dump'
git stash to pause changes while exploring alternatives
git bisect to find which generation broke something

columns.heading: Working with generated code
items: Never commit without reading the full diff
Use .gitignore to exclude AI temporary files
Tag working versions: git tag v0.1-working
Note the prompt that triggered the change in the commit message

columns.heading: Avoiding junk commits
items: Squash before merge: consolidate vibe coding sessions
Conventional Commits: feat:, fix:, refactor:, chore:
Pre-commit hooks: linting and tests before committing
Pair code review: human reviews what the AI generated

m05.title: Professional branching and AI-assisted continuous delivery
m05.content: AI + CI/CD: ask Cascade to generate the GitHub Actions workflow, Dockerfile and deploy scripts automatically.

branches.name: main
branches.desc: Stable releases only. AI never touches main directly.

branches.name: develop
branches.desc: Integration branch. AI proposes merges here.

branches.name: feature
branches.desc: One branch per feature. AI works in its own feature branch.

branches.name: hotfix
branches.desc: Urgent branch from main. AI generates the fix, human approves.

m05.title: GitFlow: branch flow and deployment by environments
m05.content: Each branch maps to an environment. Changes flow from feature → develop → QA → main, going through validation at each stage before advancing.

flow_diagram.description: Code promotion flow between branches and environments

steps.detail: The developer (with AI assistance) works on their feature branch

steps.action: Merge to develop + deploy to DEV
steps.detail: The feature is merged into develop. It is automatically deployed to the development environment for initial testing.

steps.action: Merge to QA + deploy to QA
steps.detail: If DEV tests pass, the code is merged to the QA branch. It is deployed to the QA environment for more rigorous testing and validation.

steps.action: Merge to main + deploy to PRODUCTION
steps.detail: Once validated in QA, the code is merged to main (production). It is deployed to the production environment as the stable release.

columns.heading: Why this flow?
items: Each environment acts as a quality filter
Bugs are caught before reaching production
Allows progressive validation: DEV → QA → PROD
The QA team works in an isolated and stable environment

columns.heading: Key rules
items: Never push directly to main or QA
Only promote code that passed the previous environment's tests
Merges always go in one direction: develop → qa → main
Hotfixes are the exception: they go directly from main and are backported to develop

m05.title: GitFlow: branch and environment diagram
m05.content: Complete view of the branching model. Each branch maps to an environment and changes only advance when they pass the previous environment's validation.

m07.title: How to guide AI to use YOUR technology stack
m07.content: Without context, AI picks the most popular stack on the internet. With the right prompt, it respects your technical decisions.

prompts.label: Stack Prompt
prompts.text: "Use Python 3.11 + FastAPI + SQLAlchemy + PostgreSQL. Do NOT use Django. Structure the project with Clean Architecture: domain, application, infrastructure, interfaces."

prompts.label: Architecture Prompt
prompts.text: "Design the layer diagram first before writing any code. Separate business logic from persistence. Services must not import ORM models directly."

prompts.label: Enforcing Patterns
prompts.text: "Apply DDD: entities, aggregates, value objects, repositories. Use domain events to communicate changes between bounded contexts."

m07.title: Project Kickoff Prompt Template
m07.content: Always start a new project with a structured prompt. This template ensures the AI understands your full context from the very first message.

prompts.label: 1. New Project Declaration
prompts.text:
We are starting a new project: [PROJECT NAME]
This will be a [web app / API / mobile app / CLI tool].

prompts.label: 2. Functional Description
prompts.text:
Functional description:
- The application must allow [main functionality]
- Users can [key user actions]
- The system should [expected behaviors]
- Main entities: [list entities and relationships]

prompts.label: 3. Technology Stack
prompts.text:
Technologies:
- Frontend: [React / Vue / Vanilla JS / etc.] with [CSS framework]
- Backend: [Python FastAPI / Node Express / etc.]
- Database: [PostgreSQL / MongoDB / in-memory]
- Other: [Docker, Redis, etc.]

prompts.label: 4. Technical Guidelines
prompts.text:
Technical guidelines:
- DO NOT use [excluded libs/patterns]
- All API responses must follow [format]
- Start with [entry point / folder structure]
- Error handling: [strategy]
- The app must [key non-functional requirement]

columns.heading: Why this works
items: Eliminates ambiguity from the first message
AI generates code aligned with your real constraints
Saves hours of corrections and back-and-forth

columns.heading: Pro tips
items: Be specific: 'FastAPI 0.115' beats 'Python backend'
Mention exclusions explicitly: 'Do NOT use SQLAlchemy'
Define the starting behavior: 'Start the server on port 8000'

m08.title: AI-guided programming: design before code
m08.content: The most common mistake: asking for code before agreeing on the design. AI is the best junior in the world if you know how to direct it.

columns.heading: Design First
items: Ask for class/sequence diagram before code
Validate architecture with the team
Only then ask for implementation
Avoids costly refactors

columns.heading: Tests First (TDD)
items: 'Write unit tests before the code'
Tests act as executable specification
AI implements to pass the tests
Automatic coverage from the start

columns.heading: Auto Documentation
items: 'Generate docstrings in Google/NumPy format'
README, OpenAPI spec, Mermaid diagrams
Automatic changelog from commits
Documentation always up to date

columns.heading: Role Prompting
items: 'Act as Senior Architect: review this design'
'As Security Expert: find vulnerabilities'
'As QA Engineer: write the test cases'
Change perspective based on the goal

m09.title: AI-guided debugging and refactoring
m09.content: Chain-of-thought: by asking for explicit reasoning, AI makes up to 40% fewer errors on complex problems.

prompts.label: Step-by-step Debugging
prompts.text: "Analyze this error step by step. First identify the error type, then locate the origin line, then trace the execution flow to the failure point."

prompts.label: Explained Refactoring
prompts.text: "Refactor this function. Explain each change you make and why it improves readability or performance."

prompts.label: Active Bug Hunting
prompts.text: "Act as a QA expert. Review this code looking for: logic errors, uncovered edge cases, possible null pointers and race conditions."

m10.title: Configure your workspace so AI understands everything
m10.content: AI is only as good as the context you give it. A well-documented environment multiplies the quality of responses.

columns.heading: Workspace Configuration
items: Create a CONTEXT.md at the root: describe the project, stack and conventions
Use .windsurfrules for permanent agent rules
Define key paths: src, tests, config
Include real code examples of the expected style

columns.heading: Environment Variables
items: Documented .env.example with all vars
Never pass real API key values to AI
Describe the purpose of each variable in comments
Use direnv or dotenv for automatic local loading

columns.heading: Reproducibility
items: Docker Compose for all local infrastructure
Makefiles with standard commands: make dev, make test
Pinned versions: requirements.txt, package-lock.json
devcontainer.json for identical team environments

columns.heading: Describing the Environment to AI
items: At session start: 'We are on Python 3.11, Docker, macOS ARM'
Include output of uname -a, python --version in context
Mention limitations: no internet access, no sudo
AI adapts its commands and paths to the real environment

m11.title: Generating code over real data
m11.content: AI can generate queries, migrations and transformations — but it needs to know the exact schema of your data.

columns.heading: Relational Databases
items: Pass the full DDL: CREATE TABLE users (...)
Export schema with \d+ (psql) or SHOW CREATE TABLE
Include indexes and foreign keys — AI uses them for optimal queries
Ask for explained queries: 'generate and explain each join'
Request automatic Alembic / Flyway migrations

columns.heading: External APIs
items: Pass the OpenAPI spec or a real response example
Include swagger.json or a response sample
AI generates the client, error handling and retry logic
Ask for strong typing (TypeScript types, Pydantic models)
Indicate rate limits and auth method in context

columns.heading: NoSQL and Data Warehouses
items: Show a real example document from the collection
For MongoDB: pass a representative JSON doc
BigQuery/Snowflake: share the table schema
AI suggests appropriate indexes and partitioning
Ask for schema validation if there is no native typing

m12.title: Give AI exactly the information it needs
m12.content: Context is the hidden superpower of vibe coding. The more precise it is, the more useful the response — with no hallucinations.

columns.heading: DB Schema
items: Table DDL, relationships and sample data for precise queries.

columns.heading: CSV / Excel
items: First rows + column types. AI infers the transformations.

columns.heading: JSON / Logs
items: Payload structure or a real error log. Exact bug location.

columns.heading: Legacy code
items: The full file or the relevant fragment. Ask it to respect the style.

columns.heading: Specs / RFCs
items: Requirements in text. AI converts them into user stories and code.

tips: Split large files into logical sections: models, services, routes.
Include only the context relevant to the problem — not the whole repo.
For large repos: generate an ARCHITECTURE.md and pass it first.
Context window: ~200K tokens in Claude 3.5 — use them wisely.
Prioritize the most recent code and the error stack trace.

m13.title: From requirements document to working code
m13.content: PDFs, technical specs and RFCs are the language of business. AI translates them directly into code — if you know how to ask.

pipeline.label: Upload the document
items: Functional requirements PDF
Technical RFC (e.g. RFC 7519 JWT)
External API spec
Existing user manual

pipeline.label: Ask for structured analysis
items: 'List all functional requirements'
'Identify entities and their relationships'
'Detect ambiguities and ask'
'Summarize as BDD user stories'

pipeline.label: Generate automatically
items: Code that implements the requirements
Tests that verify each user story
Mermaid architecture diagram
Generated technical documentation

m14.title: AI sees what you see — and can replicate it in code
m14.content: Multimodal LLMs understand images of interfaces, visual errors and designs — opening a completely new workflow.

columns.heading: UI Screenshots
items: Pass a Figma screenshot or the existing app
AI generates the corresponding HTML/CSS/React with high visual fidelity
"Replicate this design in React with Tailwind. Respect spacing and colour palette."

columns.heading: Visual Bugs
items: Screenshot the bug as it appears in the browser
AI identifies the affected element and proposes the CSS or logic fix
"This component overflows on mobile. Identify the problem and fix it."

columns.heading: UI Reverse Engineering
items: From an existing web or mobile app, AI deduces the component structure
"Analyze this interface and generate the React components needed to replicate it."

columns.heading: Assisted Web Scraping
items: Pass the URL or page HTML. AI generates the scraper
"Generate a Python/BeautifulSoup scraper to extract the products from this page."

m15.title: AI as technical architect of complete solutions
m15.content: The final level of vibe coding: not just generating code, but designing end-to-end solutions with trade-off analysis.

columns.heading: Full Solution Design
items: AI presents options with pros/cons
Selects the optimal one with justification
Generates the code for the chosen solution
Proposes a migration plan for existing systems

columns.heading: Trade-off Analysis
items: Automatic comparison tables
Analysis by dimension: CAP theorem, cost, DX
Quantified recommendations with reasoning
